comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's a little past midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Geoffrey Hodson, one of the leading lights of the Theosophical Society in its post-Blavatsky era. Born in 1886 in England, he served in the First World War as a tank officer, and thereafter became a pacifist, vegetarian, mystic, and clairvoyant. He and his wife used to drive around the English countryside in a motorcycle and sidecar, taking notes on the local nature spirits. They later moved to Australia and then to New Zealand, where he finally died in 1983. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 712,254th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***
[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: How skilled at magic would somebody need to be to put a whammy on someone who constantly wore a red bag amulet with salt and a bent nail, kept a saucer of vinegar evaporating by the bed, and took a weekly hoodoo bath?  Would working nasty magic on someone who took these basic precautions even be, from a practical standpoint, possible?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you were really good, you could probably get some traction against someone who did that sort of precautions, because natural magic is external and mostly works on the etheric level. If the target also did a daily protective ritual, though, you'd be wasting your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: And if a daily protective ritual would be all the target did, would it still be a waste of time?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, because there would still be some degree of etheric vulnerability. It really does help to have multiple lines of defense.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Makes perfect sense!

So, if we mix the physical plane into the picture, when a fist flies into one's face, years of daily LBRP practice and bent nail talisman on the neck are not going to be of much help. Ritual practice can reduce the likelihood of getting in a situation like this. Talisman may help with discharging the heat out of a drama, thus preventing it in some cases. However, if punch up is on, blocking, deflecting, or absorbing the fist physically is the only way to deal with it meaningfully. Having years of martial arts training would be immensely benefitial too.

Is this roughly right?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's precisely correct. As the adage goes, "the planes are discrete and not continuous;" in other words, each plane has to be addressed on its own terms, and while there are threads of connection that allow changes on one plane to influence changes on others, you've also got to act on the plane on which you want results.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I had it in my head that the protective rituals were all about the etheric level, so very much appreciate the opportunity to straighten out my thinking!

I’ve done the Heathen LBRP and Middle Pillar excercises since the day(s) you kindly made them available. (And will start the Heathen Rose Cross ritual the day it is posted here.)

It occurs to me that other than happily not missing a morning of them, I really am foggy on what, exactly, they do.

Thanks!
OtterGirl

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The LBRP balances and cleanses your aura, getting rid of astral crud and placing you in a balanced relationship to the four elements. The MP charges your astral and mental bodies with energy from the spiritual plane. The LBRP, in other words, is like taking a shower, and the MP is like having a good breakfast!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you! This is very helpful.

OtterGirl

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

After starting a new job recently I find that I'm struggling to mentally disconnect from work. Although I don't do work or answer e-mails outside of the office I find that my mind still strays onto work related thought when I'm at home. I even dream of work sometimes. Do you have any advice on how I can force work to stay at work?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It may simply be that you need a while to adjust to the new conditions. If the problem continues, a well-chosen affirmation might help.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for your advice. You might be right that I should give myself more time. Perhaps, more generally, I was wondering what the best way is to train your mind not to think thoughts you don't want. Things like negative self talk, feelings of hopelessness. I guess I was hoping for something along the lines of an opening and closing ritual, to do on the bus to work, to bookend the workday, to keep stray thoughts at bay.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The best way to do that is to give your mind other things to think about. Affirmations are the best counter for negative self-talk -- they are, after all, positive self-talk -- and you can memorize a couple and repeat them to yourself when you need to chase away negative thoughts. The other half of the cure, though, is to take in information that interests and encourages you, so your mind has plenty of positive stuff to think about in its spare time.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) Since the microcosm and macrocosm mirror each other, do you think an analysis of the Plutonian Era as a Plutonian experience on a massive scale makes sense?

2) Do you know of any sources on Ceres in astrology during the Romantic Era?

3) Taking Pluto's discovery time as a chart of Pluto's influences, western and northern Europe have Scorpio on the cusp of the second house; while Australia and New Zealand had Pluto in the second house. Japan had Pluto's discovery location in the second house in its foundation chart, and Israel had Pluto in the tenth house in its foundation chart.

All of which is to say the entire 1st world looks to have strong Plutonian energies. Are there other places where capitalism was constrained during the Plutonian Era I should look into? I think I have them covered, and it does look like capitalism's worst features can only be countered by Pluto....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's worth the attempt, certainly.

2) Nope. I've never seen anything about the astrology of Ceres' rise and fall as a planet.

3) Can you name me a location where capitalism wasn't constrained at some point between 1930 and 2006?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The U.S. during the Reagan era?

replies.title: us capitalism
replies.content:
Reagan changed the tax code, but the restrictions on banking and investment companies left from the reforms pushed through by FDR weren't fully removed until the Clinton era. Admittedly the destruction of the air traffic controllers union damaged the labor movement but federal and state labor laws were not affected, nor were many of the environmental regulations, workplace safety laws, and so forth weakened during Reagan's term. Remember that people had been frightened by the stagflation of the 70s.

Rita

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That was unclear, wasn't it? Let me try again: can you name me a location where capitalism wasn't constrained at any point between 1930 and 2006?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It occurs to me that there's actually a good case to be made here: the biosphere will never be the same after the Plutonian Era.....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not at all. Give it fifty million years, and all that will be left of the Plutonian era will be a layer of rock an eighth of an inch thick with an unusually large number of radioactive isotopes and some odd carbon compounds.

comments.title: How to interpret a pressure sensation from gods.
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Several times while invoking the name of a god I have experienced a pressure. It builds in intensity as long as I continue to invoke their name. I'm guessing that this is a common experience. I think I have also noticed some minor changes after one of these experiences. After I first invoked Esus in an attempt to get to know him I later found that I could dimly perceive the voices of trees and plants around me when I listened for it.

What does the occult literature say about invoking the blessings of gods like this. What is it described as. Calling down the power of the god,  receiving an individual blessing, opening a channel for them to manifest their divine power? Could be all sorts so ask the god?

Thank you for providing all the advice you do through here. Its great to have this as a "I'm not crazy, this is real and normal" reality check when needed.

replies.title: Re: How to interpret a pressure sensation from gods.
replies.content: When I started doing the Sphere of Protection last year (Esus is invoked at the West gate) I had several episodes of clairaudience where I heard birds chirping in a forest.  If you're crazy, so am I.

replies.title: Re: How to interpret a pressure sensation from gods.
replies.content: We may be crazy, but we are a happy band of lunatics!  🙃

replies.title: Re: How to interpret a pressure sensation from gods.
replies.content:
That's one fairly common way that people can experience nwyfre, the life force. The gods invoked by Druids, manifest very powerfully through the life force, as you might expect. Exactly what you want to call it is of course a complex thing, depending on that very unsteady instrument we call human language -- but I'd call it receiving a blessing.

You're not crazy, by the way. This is real and normal. ;-)

comments.title: Joining a Group
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have been following the practice of ritual and meditation from your book Paths of Wisdom for the last year or so. I have been wondering about the possible advantages of joining a group or order that follow the Western traditions, and if this would be a beneficial move. I have recently moved to a city where the Builders of Adytum group seem to be active. I’m wary of joining a group only to find that I am taken on a different path than I am on. Reading about the group online is not painting a sufficient picture for me to make this decision. I was wondering if you might have some guidance on 1). Joining a group in general, and 2) Joining the Builders of Adytum in particular.

replies.title: Re: Joining a Group
replies.content: If I may respond the to the second question, the BOTA typically has a public study group that anyone can go to without needing to be a member, and this will let you get a feel for it. The study group is limited entirely to material that the BOTA considers 'canon', and general discussion vis-a-vis the occult is discouraged. The symbol set in Paths is very similar. Officers are installed on or around the equinox, and the installation ritual as well as the 'vibratory attunement' ritual [typically held every other month] is open as well, so there's plenty of opportunity for sampling...

replies.title: Re: Joining a Group
replies.content:
BOTA works with the standard Golden Dawn set of correspondences, which are the ones on which Paths of Wisdom is based, so that should be no problem. Groups vary; I'd encourage you to consider signing up for BOTA's correspondence course and doing at least some of the work in it before you go ahead and join, to be sure you resonate well with its approach.

For what it's worth, I did the BOTA course many years ago and found it valuable.

comments.title: Exercices to work on the astral body ?
comments.content:
Hello,
Could you please recommend one or two good exercises to practice developing one's own capacities in terms of moving the astral body?
What kind of perceptions would this entail ?
Like some sort of dream, or more like actual sight ?
Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Exercices to work on the astral body ?
replies.content: Well, no, because I don't recommend developing the astral body apart from a more general course of magical training. That's like deciding that you're going to develop your arm muscles without strengthening any of the rest of your body. In exercise as in magic, it's healthier to start with a good general course of conditioning, and then add in specific exercises to develop this or that capacity once you've got the foundation in place.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG. Thank you for this forum.

I am starting to prepare for my first working of Geomantic magic. I have assessed that I’m stuck in a condition that seems to correspond best to Puer, though Carcer or Tristiana don’t seem far off the mark at all. Manifestations that trouble me include a deficiency of will, i.e. inconstancy and distractibility with difficulty in being prompt and productive, difficulty in addressing non-urgent priorities until they become urgent, and difficulty in empathizing or readily anticipating the feelings and perspectives of others, which may manifest, for example, as inadvertently provoking conflict with family members.

My approach would be to take the figure representing my current state, Puer, and having a goal state of Fortuna Major (with a core underlying goal being to remedy Puer’s lack of the Water element). Also, my selection of this figure is strongly influenced by the commentary in your book The Art and Practice of Geomancy where you write: “Fortuna Major represents a natural shape of events that brings success without apparent effort. Though we often think of success as a matter of vigorous action and struggle, real success comes about because our inner life is reflected in our outer circumstances… without any conscious effort at all”.

Today I cast a geomantic divination on what to expect should I undertake this working, and the result was Conjunctio in the eighth house, so I gather this is auspicious for the working I have in mind.

So to get from Puer to Fortuna Major, one must combine it with Cauda Draconis. So my gamahe would consist of a 2 inch circle of paper with a sigil of Cauda Draconic drawn on it; since all the sigils for Cauda Draconis happen to be inverted from those for Caput Draconis, I presume I would need to underline the sigil to define “down”.

In terms of timing, I would select a waxing moon as I am seeking to gain elemental Water, and more explicitly, increase Will.

My first question is: Is my thought process correct so far?
Second question: Cauda Draconis does not have a planetary correspondence. So what color do I draw the sigil with (and cover the altar with)? What incense do I suffumigate it with? And since I want it to last, I’ll want to use a herbal infusion on the paper, so what herb do I use?
Third question: Where do I place the gamahe? Since I’ll need its effects at all times, at least while I’m awake, I’m guessing I should wear it in a sachet, perhaps by the heart, or even just carry it in my shirt pocket.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first, are you sure that Puer is the figure you need? If you have a shortage of will, that's not a Puer characteristic -- Puer is overflowing with will, what he lacks is sensitivity. If you have a deficiency of will and of sensitivity both, then the figures that correspond to that would have double points in the first and third lines -- Acquisitio or Tristitia.

Second, will is fire, not water.

Third, if on reflection you decide to go with the ritual you've set out, Renaissance magical texts assign green and brown to Cauda Draconis.

Finally, you don't need to carry the sigil with you; its effects are not restricted by space. Put it in a safe location near your bed.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for this. I have a lot of food for thought.

comments.title: Protecting Against an Unstable Person
comments.content: I have a practical question. My wife and I have a flatmate who has just escaped from an abusive relationship with her partner. She is doing reasonably well considering, but her partner recently returned and smashed a window in the house. He was later arrested, and is now out on bail. There is a protection order in place against him, as well, so I think the practical material steps to protect us have been taken. We were wondering if there was any natural magic that could help protect us/the house from him. (We aren't trying to protect anyone without their permission, so that shouldn't be a concern either).

replies.title: Re: Protecting Against an Unstable Person
replies.content: Sure. Get a photo of the former partner, a bit of his hair, or a piece of paper on which he has written his name. Put it in a small glass bottle and fill the bottle with a mixture of kosher salt and cayenne pepper. Take it down to a river that flows away from your flat, toss it in, and say aloud, "(name of person), I release you into the hands of the Infinite." Then go home by a different route from the one you took to the river.

replies.title: Re: Protecting Against an Unstable Person
replies.content: If there’s no river handy, what would you use?

replies.title: Re: Protecting Against an Unstable Person
replies.content: I'd travel as far as necessary to get to a river. The spell is quite specific.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've noticed a pattern with certain attempts of mine at psychic receptiveness: I have initial success and then it completely ceases. For example, in my daily ogham readings I tried incorporating spontaneous mental imagery into my interpretation of each few (to help get away from book interpretations) The first few times this worked surprisingly well, and then it seemed to stop working. Likewise when I have tried to repeatedly guess the colour someone is thinking of I often get several right and then none right after that. It's as if the moment I have enough success that I start to expect success, I undermine it.

Is this pattern something you are familiar with? If so, is it something that one can overcome with persistence, or a sign that enough is enough? Or something else?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's extremely common -- common enough that parapsychology literature discusses it at quite some length. From an occult perspective, it's usually a sign that you haven't built the necessary foundation of skills and awareness yet. More meditation will help.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Do particular kinds of meditation themes help more than others? (in the past year I tended to do rather thought-heavy, analytical meditations, and lately they have been more sensory--do these have different effects?)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It really does vary from person to person. Try different approaches and see what works best for you.

comments.content:

Since you mentioned in the Open Post the effects of learning skills for future lives, am I right in the assumption that people who have already had many human incarnations have a broader range of talents and abilities than people who have had only a few human incarnations?

replies.title: Re: Thank you

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I had a thought on the subject of menstruation and magic, and that is simply that there might not have been a great number of female ceremonial magicians of childbearing years available to research it historically, since most women would have been living with young children during that period of life, and at least for Europe, those women who opted out of marriage and children in the last thousand years or so were likely to pursue mysticism in the monastic lifestyle judging from the few records I've encountered. I haven't gone looking, either, though.

Sometimes we modern ladies forget how much of our ancestresses' lives were spent with small children.

If there is any effect of our hormone cycle, I expect that we would also see effects from hormonal birth control to further complicate the picture.

BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: And yet there's just enough poking around in such things to make one vaguely hopeful that the research is out there. We have the idea of menstruation purging bad humors contributing to the belief in bloodletting, early notions of the uterus as a distinct animal and the various conceptualizations of hysteria that followed... it's somewhat surprising that the magical treatment would be absent given a body of speculation along other lines. My history is nowhere near solid enough to capably pursue all the various avenues of magic to see if something's been overlooked, but I'll report back if someday something turns up.

replies.title: mensturation
replies.content:
Moderns also overestimate how much time pre-modern women spent menstruating. Since periods usually cease during pregnancy and do not resume for some time after if a woman is breastfeeding exclusively the actual number of times a sexually active woman would have been in the menstrual hut or other isolation would have been relatively small. Too bad these topics were not worthy of comment in the writings that have survived--I would love to know what the routine was for the Vestal Virgins in Rome, for example. Women who live together often synchronize their cycles. Would the populace have noticed that there were certain days that the Vestals did not make an appearance. Did this affect the calendar for events that they had to preside at or appear for, such as the games?

Rita

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think that's definitely part of it, but there's also a matter of certain very deep-seated prejudices. That famous Victorian textbook, William Acton's The Functions and Disorders of the Reproductive Organs, never mentions vaginas -- "reproductive organs" meant male reproductive organs. Gynecology was a brand new thing in the mid-19th century -- there'd been a lot of it in the Middle Ages and Renaissance, but it was ignored in the early modern period for reasons of religious morality.

It wasn't until fairly recently that occult authors really started dealing with the fact that women aren't inferior men. To be fair, it wasn't until fairly recently that anyone in Western society really started dealing with that. Dion Fortune and the other occult authors of her generation were breaking new ground when they wrote about polarity, and they had to deal with constant nasty propaganda on the part of the religious mainstream, insisting that to talk about sexuality at all was tantamount to throwing "pagan orgies" et al.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I have a question about discursive meditation: is it a problem if one's knowledge about the subject on which one is meditating is limited? Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, though it helps if you can find as much information as possible.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I didn't get around to asking this last week, though it would certainly have fit with the focus on Pluto: You wrote two weeks ago that no one has managed to create a system of initiation based on the King Arthur myths because the tale ends in failure and so you would embody that failure - but what does that say about mythological systems that end in some form of apocalypse, wouldn't it be similar for them? I'm thinking especially of Norse myth. Sure, some gods and humans survive Ragnarök, but most don't and there's no mention of reincarnation for them either. Wouldn't this give a kind of "death drive" to the practitioner, an inevitable bend toward a great catastrophe? Especially since the Aesir sort of bring about their own downfall.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Norse myth has to be handled carefully; specifically, you have to be very clear about the difference between you and the gods. The problem with Arthurian initiation is that inevitably, people want to mediate the energies of the Arthurian figures, taking on their identities and roles in magical work, and the results follow as described. As long as worshipers of the Aesir and Vanir don't do this -- as long as they don't try to identify themselves with the gods -- they should be fine.

The classic counterexample, of course, is the Third Reich. Hitler deliberately identified himself with Wotan, using the famous painting of the Wild Hunt by Franz Stuck as a magical image:
[IMAGE:0]
(That was painted in 1889, by the way, the same year Hitler was born.) Having done that, he proceeded to make a beeline for his own Gotterdammerung.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you want a good scare, check out Stuck’s Medusa.  Holy nightmares, Batman!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
When one makes a Talisman, i get that you're incarnating and intention, in a suitable body, at a favourable time, favourable here meaning that the magical being in question will receive a spiritual mark that will fit well with the intention.Is that correct?

My question is, aside from the jaspberry jam effects, you're strongly vinculated to any talisman you did, and a part of your own will is commited there, unable to be used for other purposes? Or is making a Talisman "free", in terms of will?

Finally, if i make and amulet out of a Jupiter stone, and the enchant it with a Grove working from the DMH, does Jupiter has to be free from affliction in my natal chart, or it's not the same than in Golden Dawn or Astrological Talismans?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's quite correct.

2) No magical act is free in terms of your will. Once you do something with magic, your will is committed to it, and that permanently shapes who you are and what you will experience. It's not that you can't use the will for other purposes, it's that your will will always tend to move in the same direction in a similar situation.

3) That restriction is only true for talismans consecrated by astrological election, as in the Picatrix. You can do planetary Golden Dawn talismans or amulets using the DMH system whether or not the planet in question is free from affliction in your natal chart; it's wise to make sure that it's free from affliction at the time you consecrate the talisman or amulet, but that's a different matter.

comments.title: Masonic organizations
comments.content:
Thank you for this wonderful forum on dreamwidth, as well as your other blog. I've only been reading along for about six months and already feel like I've learned so much!

I know that you encourage your readers to join Masonic organizations. I am familiar with mainstream Masonic groups, having family members active in Masons and Eastern Star. I, myself, obtained majority in one of the Masonic youth organizations.

I just recently learned about Le Droit Humain or co-masonry and I'm curious to hear your take on these organizations. Opinions online are quite divided. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Masonic organizations
replies.content: Same poster here - I thought to add that I am female, so my options for which organization to join are more limited!

replies.title: Re: Masonic organizations
replies.content: Which ones do you recommend for a woman?

replies.title: Re: Masonic organizations
replies.content: As a man, I'm always a little unsure how to answer questions like that!

replies.title: Re: Masonic organizations
replies.content: Oh, sorry, I was posing the question to the lady above you.  I should have been clearer.

replies.title: Re: Masonic organizations
replies.content: Were you asking me? (I'm the original poster.) I'm not quite sure which i would recommend,as I'm still exploring myself. Eastern Star is the group I am most familiar with; it seems to be more grounded in Christianity than Freemasonary, if that impacts your decision. That said, I know people who belong to Co-Masonry, and they seem to find it quite satisfactory. If you're not eligible to join a regular Masonic body, it's something you can certainly consider.

comments.title: water spirits
comments.content:
I work in a rural area where the local college owns a rather beautiful, swampy little valley with several natural springs. The springs were the college water supply until they became too expensive to maintain. The grass in the valley is green until temperatures hit single digits in winter.
The springs have huge mature hardwood trees around them. The area was once a natural amphitheater for school plays.
The spirit of those waters has no reason to be happy with the neighbors. An elderly man wanted to renew the amphitheater, but died as the crew was finishing the cleanup of trash and dead trees around the springs. He put green plastic mesh on the ground to prevent erosion. Thorny blackberries and thick grasses are now growing through it to rip it up.
Would it be best to address such a spirit when in the woods, or leave her well enough alone? I've quietly blessed the waters on Theophany and scattered nuts for the squirrels in a bad mast year, and begged her pardon when gathering plant samples for my classes. How can one start rebuilding a relationship with Nature (and the spirits thereof) in a world trying its best to be completely divorced from all such things?

replies.title: Re: water spirits
replies.content: Talk to the spirit of the spring, and find out if she wants anything to do with you or other human beings at all. If she does, ask her what she wants. Rebuilding a relationship with Nature starts with listening to her.

replies.title: Re: water spirits
replies.content: Thank you. I think she's keeping up with things in town- several years ago a beaver came down from the woods into town, looked around, and went back. Nobody knew we had beavers. Last year a huge groundhog did the same thing, and took refuge under my house for a while. There seems to be an entire ecosystem (not exactly the right word) operating under our noses, just out of view. I am studying this quiet, hidden world and trying to learn to listen, to treat the beings in the world as subjects (to admire and learn from) rather than objects (to be manipulated). Turning off the electronics and really listening is the hard part.

replies.title: Re: water spirits
replies.content: "Ecosystem" is exactly the right word -- a small valley is amply large enough to contain an interlocking system of living things and nutrient flows -- and your attitude of quiet attentiveness is the right approach. The ancient Greeks would have called the spirit of the waters a nymph, and made offerings to her; you'll have to see if that's what she wants, though.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
The renovation of my bedroom is almost done. I think the house spirit was very enthusiastic about it, and it went easier and turned out better than I expected. Is there a good day and moon phase to move back into my room, and is there anything else I should do? I already gave the room a hoodoo floor wash when it was in a more stripped-down state.

I think I'm going to combine moving back in with starting a dream journal and making a lavender and linden dream amulet. Can that be enhanced with moon phase, day, colour of bag and candle, etc?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The waxing moon is favorable for beginnings, and any day will do. Dreamwork is ruled by the Moon; a Monday in the Moon's second quarter and a blue bag and candle would be suitable.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
When is the best moon phase to do a sweetening spell?

Similar to the sweetening spell, is there a Jupiter jar spell for success and greatness? What would go in the jar? Timing wise, this sounds like a full moon thing, is that accurate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) First quarter, as with anything you want to grow and flourish.

2) I don't know of a jar spell for that, but you can make an amulet of Jupiter containing herbs, stones, etc. associated with that planet, in a purple bag. It should not be the full moon, since that's the completion of growth; you want to make it during the Moon's first quarter, again, so your success will grow and flourish.

comments.title: Cold steel
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

(To be clear: Not asking for medical advice, only for the occult reason this may have happened.)

Do you know of any magical reasons why drawing even a very slight amount of blood with cold iron might cause someone to become unexpectedly nauseous, dizzy, near-feinting, very cold and ill?

I'd love to hear your take on this.

As always,
Yours in Druidry,
Brigyn

replies.title: Re: Cold steel
replies.content: Hmm! No, I don't.

replies.title: Re: Cold steel
replies.content: Not an occult reason: vasovagal syncope. Knew a great hulking bruiser of a rugby player with it in high school, very embarrassing for him. Don't even have to see the blood, just be able to picture it, drop like a stone, appear to go into shock.

comments.title: Babies
comments.content:
Hi Mr. Greer:
My brother who is not a very caring nor trustworthy person had twins recently, I haven't spoken to him for more than a year because he is very aggressive towards me and my kids, but I'd like to send my nephews a gift, and I was thinking of making some crochet teddy bears with an amulet inside, to Sen them my love even if I don't see them because I prefer to stay away from their dad...
Do you think it wouldn't be bad for them?
Should I wait till they get older?

replies.title: Re: Babies
replies.content: Doing magic for people without their permission is a violation of magical ethics, and should be avoided. I'd encourage you instead to use prayer -- deities are good at handling such things.

replies.title: Re: Babies
replies.content: Thanks for your answer!

comments.title: Pluto as Lord of the Damned
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I asked on a FB group if people thought that Pluto had been diminished. The answer was no, universally, although by 2036 it may be a different answer. Rick Levine had this to say:

Charles Fort wrote, "By the damned, I mean the excluded." Pluto has been excluded from the planetary club by a group of academics whose classification doesn't matter. Nevertheless, Pluto as the Lord of the Damned (those exiled to the Underworld) actually GAINS power by its exclusion or damnation. Therapy brings the excluded (the unconscious) into awareness and diminishes its power. Pluto is stronger when damned.”

Thoughts on the above?

Also, will the Pluto square Moon aspect in my natal chart have less and less influence as the years progress? I kind of hope so.

Thanks for doing MM btw – I always look forward to it.

replies.title: Re: Pluto as Lord of the Damned
replies.content:
Well, of course. My view on that subject is going to be controversial, and will probably become more so, not less, as the Plutonian current fades out. The thing I'd note is that its expressions in our collective life are very obviously fading out; psychotherapy, nuclear power, space travel, revolutionary Marxism -- all of them were the wave of the future until they weren't, and all of them have become not merely dysfunctional but also rather dowdy as time goes on.

It occurs to me that Pluto has a dimension not often recognized: it makes false promises. All four of the things I just mentioned came on the scene with grand claims about the total transformation of human life, and all four slowly petered out as those promises failed to come true.

I'd expect your Pluto square Moon to fade, though it may take a while. One of the reasons Pluto doesn't seem to have much effect on me is that it's rather weakly placed in my chart.

replies.title: Re: Pluto as Lord of the Damned
replies.content: What of the myth of progress? A perfect, Utopian society can be created by way of mechanizing everything: in other words, by eliminating nature from the world. I note it also started failing big time around the time Pluto was downgraded...

replies.title: Re: Pluto as Lord of the Damned
replies.content: Another good example.

replies.title: Re: Pluto as Lord of the Damned
replies.content: Why do you think it'll be more controversial as the Plutonian current fades out?

replies.title: Re: Pluto as Lord of the Damned
replies.content: Because that's human nature. People who are committed emotionally to something that's failing almost always double down on it, at least for a while.

replies.title: Re: Pluto as Lord of the Damned
replies.content: Thanks. It seems Pluto has a Trickster aspect that's rarely recognised.

comments.title: Planetary magic and plants
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. I hope the following question is easy to answer! I have a fair number of indoor plants who are doing very well. However, I am wondering from a planetary magic perspective if there would be any bonus or benefit to the plants if I watered them on a particular day or hour (e.g., that of the Moon) as opposed to others.

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Planetary magic and plants
replies.content: I’m not JMG, but in my 30 years of houseplant-ing I have experimented with this, and as far as I could tell, on the small scale of indoor gardening, it makes no difference.

replies.title: Re: Planetary magic and plants
replies.content: Watering them when they need water is more important than following a planetary or lunar schedule. If they're doing well, I wouldn't worry about it.

replies.title: Re: Planetary magic and plants
replies.content:
Hear, hear! Also, be extremely stingy with fertilizing, now that potting soil is loaded with fertilizer. Adding additional fertilizer may very well kill the plant.

If you do find potting soil without fertilizer, first, please write in here and let me know where you got it so I can go buy a hundred pounds or so, because you sure can’t get it anywhere around here. If our soil weren’t so darn silty I’d make up my own. Second, now you can open your bottle of liquid fertilizer. Make up a quart that is EXTREMELY DILUTED, about 1/4 the strength recommended on the label. Whenever you water the plants, add a tablespoon or two to your watering can. This steady, gentle fertilization is much closer to conditions the plant would encounter in nature than is a huge dose once a month.

Also, unless you live in a latitude of minimal winter solstice, don’t fertilize between 1November and 1 March, when the plants are sleepy. (You’ll be able to tell when they’re waking up in the spring, new shoots, and on flowering plants new buds, will start showing their little green tips.)

Have fun! This Stuff Works, too.

comments.title: Childhood memory
comments.content:
Sir;

I have a strange childhood memory and I'm curious whether you have any idea about what might have happened there:

I might have been five years of age or so, I was sitting in my room, doing nothing, when something entered the room. I couldn't really see its particulars, because it was rotating around its vertical axis very fast. It was perhaps as large as I was that time, or even a bit smaller. Very oddly, I remember I identified it as the Easter-bunny. It entered the room through the open door, moved in a little curve, then was in front of me. Then I got a hefty slap on my right cheek, after which the being left the room, never to be seen by me again.

Assuming that this really happened and it's not just a false memory of something that was originally just a dream: Does anything about this sound familiar to you, do you have an idea what this might have been?

Best regards,
Heinrich

replies.title: Re: Childhood memory
replies.content: Hmm! No, I have no idea what that might have been.

replies.title: Re: Childhood memory
replies.content: Pity for me...but I find a great asset here is that you often answer a question like this: You can not possibly know everything under the sun, so if you admit, like here, that you have no idea it adds greatly to your credibility, I find. In any case: thanks for the chance to ask questions here, Sir.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I'm wondering if a divination system such as Tarot or Runes (which I both have fluency in) can be helpful or reliable in a big move... I am from the center of fracking country, and when we moved here there was no such thing as hydrolic fracturing. So anywhere we go could potentially have something terrible come up. Or maybe there won't be anything terrible with staying in the area, where I have roots, though there has recently been a cluster of extremely rare cancer popping up in one of the more heavily fracked areas. Anyway, it seems very hard to tell where would be a good place to move and I am looking for some kind of way to see ahead.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends entirely on how good you are with those oracles. Many people use divination all the time for decisions like that one, and get good results, but since I don't know whether how far your fluency with them extends, I can't tell you for certain.

I certainly used divination (of several kinds) in my relocation to Rhode Island, and so far the results have been quite good -- but your mileage may vary.

comments.title: Research bottleneck
comments.content: What are the Tifatinian deities?

replies.title: Re: Research bottleneck
replies.content: Deities associated with a mountain in Italy. Diana Tifatina was the form of Diana worshiped by the Oscans (an Italic people absorbed by the Romans) there. You're thinking of the Aldous Huxley quote, aren't you? I'm pretty sure he simply meant by that the rural deities of Italy, who were venerated in Roman Paganism as agricultural deities.

replies.title: Re: Research bottleneck
replies.content: I'd advise checking to see if Virgil mentions them in any of his poems. He had a family farm in Campania, the part of Italy where Mount Tifata is located, and his Georgics and Bucolics (two long poems about farming and rural life) might be worth searching.

replies.title: Re: Research bottleneck
replies.content: Now I have a mental image of a slanted font of italic people being taken over by the square uprights of a Times New Roman. Thanks for the leads, though!

replies.title: Re: Research bottleneck
replies.content: That's actually not a bad description of what happened to Italy over the several centuries it took Rome to conquer it!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
As always, thanks for Magic Monday. I appreciated your response last week to my heebie-jeebies about old sailing ships, and the idea I may have drowned below decks once - it certainly explains why I can hardly watch those people who go diving in underwater caves! Water+enclosed spaces = no for me, thanks.

It once again reinforced the message I've read here many times, that we should listen to those "odd" sensations and fascinations in our lives. Which brings me to my question this week, that I've actually been sitting on since I started reading here months ago...

You mentioned once that as a person gets into magic and opens up to higher beings,"the pantheon may choose you." I've found my thoughts drifting back to ancient Egypt again and again, first with Bastet and my cats, then popping up here and there. When I was in my early teens (like the ship thing!) I had a pretty serious ancient Egypt phase - I bought an ankh at a mall kiosk and wore it every day, drew Thoth and Ra on my binder, read all the Egyptian mythology books I could find. My Presbyterian upbringing soon drew me back to my surrounding cultural norms, but the fondness for those old gods stayed with me - I even had a scarab with a sun disk tattooed on my forearm several years ago, because the meaning resonated with me at the time.

Way back in the late 90's, I received an advertisement in the mail from Time-Life books, about a new history series they were putting out (no money down, cancel anytime) and the first 50 people to subscribe would each receive a small collection of ancient Egyptian artifacts. I thought the books sounded neat and I could afford them at the time, and why not try for the artifacts? Lo and behold, I was one of the first 50, and received two lapis scarabs, two faience Eyes of Horus, and a larger bronze ankh, all at least 2,000 years old, with a certificate of authenticity from a NYC gallery and everything.

I never usually "win" anything. It still startles me that I was one of the first 50 to send back that postcard, from my little rural town.

Over the past 20 years or so I've kept these artifacts very respectfully, for as far as I know someone sometime in the distant past was buried with them. I had them on display in a frame for a year or two, but since then they have been stored out of sight in a keepsake box. As soon as I started reading about the serious reality of magic on your sites, I thought of them, and wondered if they are "just things" now, or if they were at some point consecrated, if I should still have them, what I should do with them, etc. I am very conscious and critical of the fact that Western Europeans went into Egypt and stole quite a lot of very sacred stuff, and yet these also ended up with me by pure chance.

Is this a matter of, "Get them out and ask them?" As always, I appreciate your thoughts and insights.

Diane

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd definitely encourage you to get them out and ask them. You may have a connection with the deities in question, and if so, if you decide to follow that up, I suspect you'll get a definite and positive response.

comments.title: Hoodoo
comments.content:
I was in the very center of moving activities last Monday and wasnt able to catch back up with you then, but just wanted to let you know that your hoodoo floor wash was absolutely amazing. I now have some idea of what "blank" actually means.

Makes me wonder...was it just me, and a new-found recognition of non-material planes, that made the difference? Would I have been able to discern a difference without a change in worldview? Our caretaker at the old homestead did it too, and was blown away by it. But she's much more in touch with that sort of thing. Very cool.

On a related note, do brownie type beings typically move with their people or stay in their physical places? Our caretaker says she thinks "our" brownie is still there, but I have seen some tell-tale etheric activity at the new place in the last week that makes me question that idea. Not skilled enough to distinguish between different house fays yet, and I'm curious about your take.

Thank you!
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo
replies.content: I think it can go either way. One Russian folklorist back in the 1840s recorded a very interesting traditional Russian ritual to bring a family;'s house spirit (in Russian, domovoi) along when a family moved into a new house.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo
replies.content:
Even people who don't have magical experience tend to respond to a space that's had a hoodoo floor wash by saying "wow, this really feels clean" and things like that. The more magic you practice, of course, the more clearly you sense such things.

House spirits vary. Sometimes they move, sometimes they don't, but a family that treats house spirits well can fairly often expect a new one to show up when they arrive at a new house, bright-eyed and ready to be friendly.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo
replies.content:
That IS good news!

My self from 5 years ago is cringing across space-time as I say this, but the thing that bothered me most about moving was leaving our house fay (spirit?) behind.

Another interesting note about our Hoodoo cleanse is that my wife was experiencing vertigo at the top of the stairs before, but after the wash that sensation was greatly diminished. Any thoughts about what that might be? Something like a replay haunting from a past accident maybe?

Thanks as always.
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo
replies.content: Quite possibly yes. You may want to redo the floor wash a few times to get rid of every trace of the sensation.

replies.title: Re: alchemy
replies.content: That depends very much on what kind of alchemy your friend has in mind. Are we talking glassware, lab goggles, strange substances in jars and bottles? In that case Mark Stavish's The Path of Alchemy is probably your friend's best bet.

replies.title: Re: alchemy

comments.title: Counters to hypnosis?
comments.content:
I feel like part of my brain habitually shuts off when someone is trying to pull wool over my eyes and try to get me to fall into their rythym and not looking at what they say too closely.

What is a good habit I can practice to sharpen my descernment when confident people try to trick me into falling into their games?

replies.title: Re: Counters to hypnosis?
replies.content:
If they try to look you in the eyes, stare fixedly at the point between their eyebrows and think, as forcefully as you can, the word "No."

The regular practice of a protective ritual such as the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram or the Sphere of Protection will take care of that more generally.

comments.title: House Ghost
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
We seem to have a very gentle male house ghost. Some clairvoyant friends have seen a tall man wearing a cape (I suspect it is a great coat) and he just said hello, a few days ago, to a very sensitive empath friend.

I have included him in my prayers and asked him to communicate with me if he needs anything. I am wondering why he is here instead of getting on with his affairs. Is there something more I can do for him?

comments.title: complex workings
comments.content:
Archdruid,

If I wanted to do a complex working with multiple interconnected, and mutually supportive intentionalities, would it be better to the working a piece at a time by focusing on one intention at a time, or weave them all into an abstract symbol like a rune and focus on the symbol?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: complex workings
replies.content: Depends on how experienced you are. A really experienced and capable mage can work in a series of complex intentions into a single magical action. A less experienced mage should do them one at a time.

replies.title: Re: complex workings
replies.content: Cool, guess I'll stick with simple for now. Out of curiosity though, is my idea of weaving multiple intentions into a single abstract symbol accurate? For some reason I've been thinking of a portrait similar to tarot cards as focus points for multiple intentions.

replies.title: Re: complex workings
replies.content: It's something that can be done, yes, and complex images -- like memory images, with each detail representing something -- are a classic way to do it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I had a dream last night, and I know you don't do dream interpretations, but I'm curious if there's anything in occult lore that might help with interpreting it. I suspect it's meant far more symbolic than literal, but anything that might help would be greatly appreciated! I expect I need to spend several meditation sessions on it as well.

Here it is: I was in a balcony, watching the gods, they were sitting at a table, which I somehow knew was the Earth. On it, there was all sorts of movement, and I couldn't remotely keep up with it, but the seven of them could: the sun, moon, Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn. Uranus came to join, and the gods were discussing what to do about certain trends they saw among humans.

I saw the dark clouds from the industrial revolution getting underway, and the gods were getting somewhat concerned: the pursuit of power and wealth was getting in the way of what we were meant to be doing.

So, a beautiful woman clad in green and blue (Ceres?) came and joined, to show us a different way. Neptune joined, and Ceres, left, and then the gods watched as we went in the opposite direction, back to pursuit of wealth and power.

Finally, Mars suggested giving us exactly what we wanted, so we could see why it wasn't a good idea. There was silence for a good long while, before the gods began discussing it. The only way to do this was to invite Pluto to the table. Venus was strongly opposed to this, but eventually the gods held the vote, and narrowly voted to invite him.

Neptune went to get Pluto, and kept a close eye on him. I saw the dark clouds billow, getting thicker and thicker; two blinding flashes of light; and the green that had covered the table turn brown. Eventually, Pluto finally said "If they haven't gotten the message yet, they never will," and got up and left.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Interesting. It seems relatively self-explanatory.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It is quite self-explanatory on one level: symbolically it makes perfect sense. There's lots of other symbolism I need to meditate on in it too. What I'm wondering is how literally can I interpret it? Is there anything in occult lore I can look into to try to figure that out?

It may ultimately be futile, but if it's literal it could explain a few things (the population explosion would make sense: the only way to reach all of us would be to force the population up a lot, for example).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Meditate on it!

comments.title: Vegan, Roses, and Tentacular Agrippa
comments.content:
Quick vegan update from a few weeks ago: after reading your response to another vegan, regarding symptoms of Plant-Based Diet Magical Hypersensitivity Syndrome (PBD-MHS), I started questioning whether I was having this issue after all. I then did an Ogham reading, and got my most clear, TSW, response I have ever gotten, saying that I should definitely continue with my magical practice and my diet. So I have, with no ill effects thus far. Yay for divination and self-reflection!

Question: I read in NME that the Rose is the West's equivalent to the richly symbolic Lotus in the East. I found this very evocative for some reason, even though I've never had much interest in roses until now. I keep picturing a twilit winter scene from some sort of fairy tale, with roses as the central aspect, and a very strange emotive atmosphere... Do you have any recommended reading to delve further into roselore?

Question: Last month, I read your Art and Practice of Geomancy, as well as your article in Magical Education about Renaissance magic, to start whetting my appetite for a possible Eldritch magical system. I'm very curious if you had any inklings of how such a system might incorporate a different symbolic system. Ritual magic, in comparison, seems to cry out for new and interesting incarnations within different symbolic systems, and it's not entirely clear to me how a similar thing might be done with Renaissance magic.

replies.title: Re: Vegan, Roses, and Tentacular Agrippa
replies.content:
1) See if you can find a copy of The Rose-Garden Game by Eithne Wilkins.

2) To do eldritch magic in a Renaissance mode, you'd either have to assign the Great Old Ones to the planets, or you'd have to use the grimoire tradition as a model. In either case, yeah, it's not so easy.

Though the planetary symbolism isn't too hard:
Moon: Phauz
Mercury: Nyarlathotep
Venus: Yhoundeh
Sun: Hastur
Mars: Ithaqua
Jupiter: Cthulhu
Saturn: Shub-Ne'hurrath
Uranus: Yog-Sothoth
Neptune: Nodens
You'd have the advantage of a specific divine symbolism for Uranus and Neptune, which would enable those planets to be used to make talismans and gamahes, too.

replies.title: Re: Vegan, Roses, and Tentacular Agrippa
replies.content: I have such a bad case of "ooh-what-a-cool-book-itis".....

replies.title: Re: Vegan, Roses, and Tentacular Agrippa
replies.content: It's a very cool book.

replies.title: Re: Vegan, Roses, and Tentacular Agrippa
replies.content:
Thanks for these. I'll see if I can find Wilkins' book. For some reason, I think roses should have some connection to Tsathoggua- meditation time!

Regarding Eldritch Renaissance- I like the inclusion of Uranus and Neptune. Actually, I was thinking what a "forged" ancient Hyperborean Aklo manuscript might contain, and one of the things that popped into my head was something similar to both the Kyranides and the Picatrix, but with a 9-planet system, instead of 7, since presumably the Great Old Ones and their followers would be aware of the non-visible planets. Over millennia, this info would have been lost to the wider public, and condensed into our remaining traditions.

Which leads me to comment on my interest in Eldritch Renaissance magic. I think such a system would be great for the future, both preserving and salvaging Western traditions, but also preserving discoveries and innovations from more recent years, and combining them into an "ecosophic" worldview. That's why I like the Great Old Ones- they are powerful and archetypal, like all gods, but they are also profoundly nonhuman. Sorry if I've mentioned this before.

I wonder- if the Renaissance and Medieval European cosmological worldview was of the universe as a perfect, contained Cathedral (or as I like to think- a Snow globe), and the Modern Western cosmological worldview is of Space and emptiness, what are possibilities for a future worldview? Maybe the universe as Organism, where every part is connected to every other part. Or even nested organisms, with the greatest Organism the entirety of the universe. I know these ideas will develop on their own over the next millennia or so, but we might as well have fun experimenting with the possibilities! Any thoughts on this?

Thanks once again for this forum!

replies.title: Re: Vegan, Roses, and Tentacular Agrippa
replies.content:
1) You're probably thinking of the potpourri oil Jenny used at the shrine of Tsathoggua in Kingsport, which included oils of rose, myrrh, galingal, and just a bit of cinnamon leaf. Oh, hadn't I mentioned that? ;-)

2) Good. I'd expect to see a nine-pointed star show up here and there in the symbolism.

3) The future will decide its own worldview, and what it chooses would probably be shocking and baffling to us if we knew it. Certainly the eldritch worldview as I've invented it in my fiction sees the cosmos as an organism -- a vast, gelatinous, pulsating organism with many tentacles and many eyes, surrounded by the music of high shrill flutes and the intricate rhythms of drumming...

comments.title: Saturn's day & hour
comments.content: I saw you mention that Saturn's day & hour were good for submitting a tax return and making that be the end if it. I had been procrastinating about submitting my 2017 return (Irish date of reckoning was 31 October 2018). When I read that I made a solemn promise to myself to get all out of the way last Saturday. I did my 2017 accounts and then I carried on and did the 2018 accounts and at Saturn's hour of 20.30 I submitted both returns, one late, one early.  I was delighted to find that although there was a late penalty, there was still a net rebate for me, and hopefully that bit of bureaucracy has gone to bed for another year. Thanks for the advice and the impetus it gave me. :)

replies.title: Re: Saturn's day & hour
replies.content: Delighted to hear it. We got our taxes in toward the end of January, timing the submission astrologically, and due to the Trump administration's income tax reforms, came in several thousand dollars ahead. TSW... ;-)

Sincerely

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You probably need to balance yourself out with some earth energy. Instead of hugging a tree, sit or stand for a little while with your back against it. An old healthy pine or fir tree is the best choice for this.

comments.title: Bindings
comments.content:
Having finished Chorazin yesterday, I got to wondering about bindings on the level of what was performed on Yhoundeh. Is there an actual basis for binding gods or other powerful beings into places or objects, or was that literary license?

If it's possible with gods, though, that would mean it's possible with humans? It seems like the kind of thing one would want to protect against.

-Jean-Pierre

replies.title: Re: Bindings
replies.content: Along with Fenriss/Fenrir, some sources recount Loki being bound by the Aesir after one too many pranks.

replies.title: Re: Bindings
replies.content: You know, I can't believe I didn't think of the mythological sources for that, but my brain was in "hard-defined ritual" mode.

replies.title: Re: Bindings
replies.content:
It's based on several incidents from mythology, like much of what's in these books. As Nyarlathotep hinted (and later books will explain), what happened was beyond anything human beings can do unaided.

A human soul can be bound into a place under very specific conditions, but it usually requires the active cooperation of the human being while alive. No, I'm not going to go into the details!

replies.title: Re: Bindings
replies.content:
The mythological route escaped me, and I forgot Nyarlathotep's hint there.

Josephine McCarthy has mentioned willingly interred sleepers who mediate between their people and the land, not with any details (it's not a process I would want to undergo or perform on anyone ever), but I suppose as the chance of it being unwillingly performed is low to none, that's good.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Bindings
replies.content:
1) More mythology for you! Reading an abundance of myths is among the best training I know of for magic.

2) Ms. McCarthy knows her stuff.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What are the magical or energy body effects of getting a vasectomy?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: None, as far as I know. William Butler Yeats, who was a senior adept of the Golden Dawn as well as a Nobel Prize-winning poet, had that done, and it didn't slow him down at all.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have been practicing magic daily for about 6 years. I recently (about 3 months ago) got a vasectomy. I have noticed no difference in the performance of, or the results of my ritual magic.
As a matter of fact before the surgery I decided that no matter what I was not going to stop my ritual regimen because I find the rituals...well beautiful, and this seemed to make my ritual practice stronger because I wasn't putting so much energy into worrying about if they worked or not.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What would you recommend a newbie to magic to try in order to prove to herself TSW?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I wouldn't. That kind of dabbling rarely has any good results at all.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I've been trying to understand Jung's active imagination, my goal was to improve, if that's the word, my dream life. It seems similar to, but different than, the discursive meditation I already do. In some ways, it seem like Jung took the occult out of occult-style discursive meditation and renamed it. But in other ways it seems maybe a bit different. I'm assuming that, since Jung was an occultist, that there's probably no harm in doing this while also doing GD stuff as well? But I thought I'd post here and see if you or anyone else had any experience with active imagination?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In the Golden Dawn literature, active imagination is called "scrying in the spirit vision," and it's an important part of the training. You'll find detailed instructions in any good book on GD magic.

comments.title: Dissociation Follow-up
comments.content:
Last week I commented on a recurring awareness of dissociation I've had since childhood:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/48445.html?thread=3433277#cmt3433277

In thinking about your follow-up question, I realized that while I have tended to think of the dissociation as being relative to "the world," it is equally relative to "my self". That is, while I don't experience anything specific to my body in these moments, I feel as out of my self as I do out of the world.

Also worth mentioning is that the whole thing makes me think of something I remember reading of yours where you said that one of the reasons magic is contraindicated for young teenagers is that magic works to bring the magician further along the path of return, and an adolescent is still working to manifest fully. So if an adolescent practices magic it can interfere with them fully manifesting.

Now aside from a few instances of the LBRP here and there, I didn't practice magic as an adolescent. But from age 7 to about age 23, I was in a lot of therapy and was heavily (sometimes very heavily) medicated. I know for certain that this interfered with the normal process of me growing up, and wonder if it also interfered with the normal process of me fully manifesting.

Worth mentioning is that magical practice in general seem to reduce the feeling of dissociation, not increase it. And again, the recurring sensation/awareness, while significant, is infrequent. It's mostly a curiosity at this point, though I suspect understanding may be of great value to me.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Dissociation Follow-up
replies.content: Spending your entire youth in a medicated haze will do that! If magic helps you get more grounded, excellent.

comments.title: Anxiety
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,
Long time listener first time caller.
Do you know any magical exercises to help with anxiety. I have suffered from regular anxiety attacks of varying intensity for most of my adult life.

From a magical perspective, what are anxiety attacks? Assuming they occur when there is no obvious trigger.

replies.title: Re: Anxiety
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, magic isn't a medical or a psychological treatment. It's a way of life and a comprehensive spiritual discipline. If you have problems with anxiety, you probably need to find a good therapist who will help you get to what's causing them.

As I understand them, anxiety attacks are caused by unresolved memories from childhood or previous lives, involving strong emotion or trauma. Anything that comes too close to the circumstances surrounding the memory will set off an anxiety attack, because it brushes against emotions you haven't yet dealt with. The attacks go away once you deal with the memories and the emotions that surround them.

replies.title: Re: Anxiety
replies.content: thank you.

comments.title: Talisman Intentions
comments.content:
I'm trying to formulate intentions for the talisman(s) I'll be enchanting during the sixth grade of the Dolmen Arch. Having never really done practical magic before, I'm unsure of the best way to formulate these intentions.

I'm considering one for general household protection and blessing, but am unsure if I should pick only one (either protection or blessing). Is including both too broad? And which cantref would be best? Spirit Above has obvious protective qualities, but I am also thinking about Earth.

I am also considering one to increase my confidence, but am worried that the obvious wording of "increase my confidence" runs the risk of making me over-confident. What would be a better wording?

Lastly I am considering one for healing my body of a few ailments, all of which share the common thread of "aberrant growths." But I want this talisman to not only remove the present growths, but also prevent new growths from appearing. Are these two separate intentions, or is "heal my body of aberrant growths" adequate for both?

I realize I may be over-thinking this, but since I've never done this kind of magic, I want to be careful.

Thanks!

(also, I forgot to log-in for the above comment about dissociation. It was also from me)

replies.title: Re: Talisman Intentions
replies.content:
Yes, you're overthinking it, but that's not a crisis. ;-)

1) "Protection and blessing" quite often go together in spells, so you can combine them in this working. I'd use Spirit Above.

2) You could always say "increase my confidence to a healthy and balanced degree" or something like that.

3) That intention sounds good to me.

comments.title: Extinction Rebellion?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I love that you do this, thank you.

My question is about Extinction Rebellion: a response to the ecological crisis we find ourselves in. Nick-named “XR” it aims to do upset the status quo.

Have you heard of it? From a magical viewpoint, do you have any thoughts? Re Rasberry Jam effect etc...
I find the words hard to stand by, but they seem to be galvanising lots of support. Any idea how it might be a force for useful change?

replies.title: Re: Extinction Rebellion?
replies.content: No doubt. I wonder if any of these knuckleheads has actually thought through what "zero emissions and drawdown by 2025" would entail in practice. Without fossil fuel inputs for agriculture and long-distance food transport, for example, Britain could support only a small fraction of its current population; are the people supporting this project ready to volunteer to be machine-gunned in batches of 200 or so, in order to help get the population down to the size the land can support? No, I didn't think so.

replies.title: Re: Extinction Rebellion?
replies.content: Wow, ouch. Straight to the point! Thank you

replies.title: Re: Extinction Rebellion?
replies.content:
An astonishingly large number of people who think they understand the ecological predicament we're in have never grasped the most basic fact of that predicament, which is that the carrying capacity of this planet is much less than the current population -- and the unsustainable use of fossil fuels and other nonrenewable resources is the only thing making that population overshoot possible.

As those resources deplete, accordingly, the Earth's human population is going to go down. Please note that I'm not saying that it "should" go down; I'm saying that it will go down, one way or another. If the people supporting this project want to make a difference, they can do it by not having children, reducing their resource footprint sharply, and pushing for political changes that ensure that as much food as possible is grown in Britain itself rather than being imported from the far corners of the world. (Yes, that means tariffs and trade barriers...)

comments.title: Nausea Cells Salts and Past Life Memories
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Thank you very much for hosting these Q&A sessions, it's been very interesting to read through past discussions. This is something I hear pretty much nothing about in the rest of my life, so it's appreciated.

I have two questions for you. The first is about cell salts. My father suffers from chronic nausea (as well as a whole host of other issues as he has kidney disease and his health has been declining drastically in the last few years) which makes it hard for him to get enough to eat. We have been only marginally successful in predicting what foods will set him off, and his doctors have not been able to give us any information. I was wondering if you (or anyone else) knows if the cell salt for nausea is effective only for flu-style nausea, or if it might treat a wider range of problems. The nausea medication that he is on right now is only somewhat effective, and (no surprise) has a whole lot of other unpleasant side effects. However my father is pretty skeptical about anything homeopathic, so I would rather not bring it up unless it has a chance of working.

My second question is personal. I've been reading you describe the experience of remembering things about your past life, and I was wondering if you think this might be something of the sort. When I was little, like 5 or 6 or so, I used to have nightmares pretty consistently about losing my parents to drug abuse. The thing I remember most is that while they were pretty hazy on the details, the emotion was very intense and always centered on the experience of having someone I loved (my parents) change dramatically, going from someone who loved me to someone who didn't recognize me and would do whatever they needed to to get what they wanted, regardless of who it affected. The dreams faded out after several years, but I always thought that was a bit weird because of their intensity, especially because drugs were never a particularly important topic when I was growing up. I lived in a small rural town, and I never saw anyone on drugs, and my real life parents never made a big deal about them either. I can't think of any reason why I would have been terrified of them. To this day I still have an intense aversion to any sort of mind-altering substance, going so far as to be cautious with caffeine and uncomfortable personally using relatively normal drugs like alcohol or marijuana. Whenever I see storyline that centers around losing someone to any form of addiction, it always feels like a gut punch, and hits home much more than it should given that I've never experienced anything of the sort.

So, I guess my question is, do you think that's the sort of feeling that might arise because of a past life experience, or did I just fixate on this when I was young for some unknown reason?

Thanks again,
Cym

replies.title: Re: Nausea Cells Salts and Past Life Memories
replies.content:
1) Prescribing cell salts requires a certain amount of practice with a good repertory (a book that lists detailed symptoms and the cell salts that go with them). Especially in a situation like this, where you've got multiple health challenges to deal with, you'd want to work with a competent practitioner who can work through the complexities of the condition. Of course there's also the nocebo effect: if a patient is convinced that something isn't going to work, its chances of working -- even if it's a pharmaceutical drug -- go way down.

2) That sounds very much like the kind of thing that indicates a past life issue. Traumatic memories sometimes surface in childhood, in particular.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
When I was a young boy I would visit my grandmother during the summer for several days. She was a bit eccentric in many ways, not least because she was a practicing occultist. Born in 1910 it seems likely she was familiar with Theosophy and was almost certainly a Rosicrucian evidenced by books on the subject in her book case.
She had a small, simple house where the living room was the only room large enough for circumambulation and this is where I slept. It was there that I encountered an incredibly malefic entity that terrified me beyond words.
Flash forward fifty years to where I am working through your LRM system, in the chapter Know Thyself, when this memory presents itself in a more understandable way. Along with the memory came the very clear impression that I was damaged in some way with the result being that I was/am host to a parasite like larvae of the Roman religion. As I became more aware and rejected this, I really have no words for it, I became very sick and anxious. I aged very quickly in a matter of weeks and worried those closest to me a great deal.
Medically, I am fine and by being diligent in prayer, banishing and the Middle Pillar feel like I'm recovering. Tarot divination is clear that I should continue my practice.
I'm sure my grandmother never intended to be careless like most and meant no harm. Your warnings about practice near children need to be honored to the letter.
I am adding the hoodoo bath weekly and will do the florida water floorwash again. Do you have any other recommendations for a novice?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Each morning, fill the bathroom sink with cold water and wash your whole body with a cloth wrung out in the water. This is in addition to any other bathing you choose to do. That's an effective way to strip off contaminated etheric energy, and it will make your energy body very uncomfortable for critters.

One of the disadvantages of some of the old occult schools is that they didn't teach any means of banishing. Depending on which Rosicrucian order she belonged to -- do you recall any of her books on that subject? -- she may actually have been discouraged from learning such things. Not a helpful habit!

comments.title: sacred sites
comments.content:
Hello John Michael,

I once visited an Aboriginal (Australian) sacred site as a tourist. The result was like walking through molasses, tunnel vision, and audible spirits atop the rocks. The spirits were not angry (bad white fella, go away!), they just wanted to know why I was there. I did not know why I was there and I was quite overwhelmed. I refused to visit the next day's sacred site (I was on a group tour) and have avoided other people's sacred sites since then.

Does occult literature have a way of understanding this? Thank your for Magic Mondays.

Live from Tidal Reach,
Chris

replies.title: Re: sacred sites
replies.content: It sounds to me as though what happened is exactly what it felt like, i.e., you ran into a bunch of spirits, realized that you had no business being there, and did the sensible thing. Messing with powerful earth energies is not for everyone!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

You and a few commenters touched on this on the other blog, but the conversation was mixed up with a million other threads. When a person takes up a practice - writing, music, gardening, chess, etc., is that anything that carries over into future incarnations?
For instance, I write as a hobby, with the intent of learning a craft, rather than making any kind of living. Is this a function of my current personality, or am I making investments in imagination, memory and willpower that will pay off much further down the road?

I also had a question about the demotion of Pluto:
Is this a matter of its influence disappearing completely, or rather becoming a minor theme in human affairs?

I suppose what's tripping me up is that we know, from the Greek myths, that Hades was an influence long before the planet was ever discovered. So there's some nuance here I'm not quite grasping, between the influence of a deity, a fundamental feature of human psychology, and the influence of a planet.

Thanks,
Cliff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) What happens is that talents, dispositions, and qualities of character get built into the structure of the higher self and carried over into later lives. Your current personality benefits, but in your next life you'll have an easier time learning to write, and the qualities of imagination, memory, and will you develop will remain with you.

2) A planet constellates a pattern of consciousness -- that is, gives it an anchor in the various planes of form and brings it into more focused expression. Before Pluto was discovered, its energies were dispersed, some under Mars, some under Saturn, a few under Venus. As it fades out, those same energies will disperse again -- though it's an interesting question whether they'll return to the same planets.
